title: Access Level Sizing Guide
summary: Understand how roles, areas, schedules, and door groups combine into access-control complexity.

sections.title: What this tool does
body: This tool estimates the structural complexity of an access-control design. It helps identify whether the proposed access model will stay manageable as the system grows.
bullets: Use it as a planning and sanity-check aid.
Use it to document assumptions before implementation.
Use it to catch design pressure before it becomes field trouble.

sections.title: When to use it
body: Use it before building access levels, during expansions, or when cleaning up an access-control system that has become hard to audit.
bullets: Early design planning.
Expansion review.
Existing-system cleanup or validation.
Explaining tradeoffs to a client, stakeholder, or internal team.

sections.title: Input descriptions
body: These descriptions explain what each input represents in practical access-control terms.

items.label: Roles
items.description: Groups of users with similar access needs, such as employees, managers, janitorial staff, warehouse staff, IT, or visitors.
items.whyItMatters: More roles usually means more permission combinations to manage.
items.commonMistake: Creating a new role for every small exception instead of grouping users by operational need.

items.label: Areas
items.description: Logical zones or departments that need separate access treatment.
items.whyItMatters: More areas increase the role-area combinations in the access model.
items.commonMistake: Splitting the building into too many tiny zones when simpler groupings would work.

items.label: Door Groups
items.description: Sets of doors managed together because they share similar access behavior.
items.whyItMatters: More door groups increase the number of permission combinations to document and audit.
items.commonMistake: Creating a separate group for every small exception.

items.label: Schedules
items.description: Time windows that control when access is allowed or restricted.
items.whyItMatters: Schedules add another layer of logic and can make access behavior harder to predict.
items.commonMistake: Using many overlapping schedules instead of a few clear operating windows.

items.label: Concurrency
items.description: How many users or access cases may operate under the same model at the same time.
items.whyItMatters: Higher concurrency makes mistakes more visible because more people depend on the model.
items.commonMistake: Testing with a small group and forgetting full operating scale.

items.label: Special Rules
items.description: Exceptions or restrictions that do not fit the normal access pattern.
items.whyItMatters: Exceptions are where access systems often become difficult to support.
items.commonMistake: Solving every exception with a permanent access rule instead of keeping a clean baseline.

sections.title: How to read the results
body: Healthy means the model is likely manageable. Watch means the design should be reviewed before implementation. Risk means the access model may become difficult to maintain or troubleshoot.
bullets: Healthy: the design appears manageable based on the entered assumptions.
Watch: the design may still work, but the assumptions deserve review.
Risk: simplify the design, add capacity, or validate the design more carefully before implementation.

sections.title: Common mistakes
body: Most access-control design problems come from underestimating how hardware, users, schedules, exceptions, and maintenance effort interact over time.
bullets: Designing only for the initial install instead of the system lifecycle.
Ignoring exceptions, expansion, or administrative workload.
Assuming manufacturer capacity limits are the same as practical design limits.
Failing to document assumptions before the system is built.

sections.title: Planning assumptions
body: This guide explains the planning logic behind the tool. It does not replace manufacturer documentation, platform-specific limits, code review, or project-specific engineering judgment.
bullets: Manufacturer limits still matter.
Local code and life-safety requirements still apply.
Site conditions may change the final design.
A simpler design is usually easier to support, audit, and explain.

sections.title: Professional responsibility
body: ScopedLabs tools are planning aids. They help surface assumptions and risk, but they do not replace formal engineering review, code compliance review, manufacturer validation, or project-specific design responsibility.
bullets: Validate against the actual product and platform being installed.
Confirm owner requirements and operating procedures.
Document assumptions before implementation.
